news_summary: Today's space news celebrates the successful return of Artemis II astronauts, advances in understanding gravitational waves, and new insights into the nature of dark matter, signaling a promising new era of space exploration and discovery.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité spatiale d'aujourd'hui met à l'honneur le retour réussi des astronautes de la mission Artemis II, les progrès réalisés dans la compréhension des ondes gravitationnelles et les nouvelles découvertes sur la nature de la matière noire, annonçant ainsi une nouvelle ère prometteuse pour l'exploration et la découverte spatiales.
news_summary_es: Las noticias espaciales de hoy celebran el exitoso regreso de los astronautas de la misión Artemis II, los avances en la comprensión de las ondas gravitacionales y los nuevos conocimientos sobre la naturaleza de la materia oscura, lo que augura una nueva y prometedora era de exploración y descubrimiento espacial.

articles.title: NASA Welcomes Record-Setting Artemis II Moonfarers Back to Earth
articles.summary: The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight. NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down at 5:07 p.m. PDT Friday off the coast […]
articles.content:
NASA’s Orion spacecraft with Artemis II crewmembers NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, commander; Victor Glover, pilot; Christina Koch, mission specialist; and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen, mission specialist aboard was seen as it splashed down at 5:07 p.m. PDT in the Pacific Ocean off the coast of California, Friday, April 10, 2026. NASA’s Artemis II mission took Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen on a 10-day journey around the Moon and back to Earth. Credit: NASA/Joel Kowsky

The first astronauts to travel to the Moon in more than half a century are back on Earth after a record-setting mission aboard NASA’s Artemis II test flight.

NASA astronauts Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, and Christina Koch, and CSA (Canadian Space Agency) astronaut Jeremy Hansen splashed down at 5:07 p.m. PDT Friday off the coast of San Diego, completing a nearly 10-day journey that took them 252,756 miles from home at their farthest distance from Earth.

“Reid, Victor, Christina, and Jeremy, welcome home, and congratulations on a truly historic achievement. NASA is grateful to President Donald Trump and partners in Congress for providing the mandate and resources that made this mission and the future of Artemis possible,” said NASA Administrator Jared Isaacman. “Artemis II demonstrated extraordinary skill, courage, and dedication as the crew pushed Orion, SLS (Space Launch System), and human exploration farther than ever before. As the first astronauts to fly this rocket and spacecraft, the crew accepted significant risk in service of the knowledge gained and the future we are determined to build. NASA also acknowledges the contributions of the entire NASA workforce, along with our international partners, whose expertise and commitment were essential to this mission’s success. With Artemis II complete, focus now turns confidently toward assembling Artemis III and preparing to return to the lunar surface, build the base, and never give up the Moon again.”

After splashdown in the Pacific Ocean, the astronauts were met by a combined NASA and U.S. military team that assisted them out of the spacecraft in open water and transported them via helicopter to the USS John P. Murtha for initial medical checkouts. The crew members are expected to return to NASA’s Johnson Space Center in Houston on Saturday, April 11.

During their mission, Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen flew 694,481 miles in total. Their lunar flyby took them farther than any humans have ever traveled before, surpassing the previous distance record set by Apollo 13 astronauts in 1970.

The first Artemis crew launched on NASA’s SLS rocket at 6:35 p.m. April 1, from Launch Pad 39B at the agency’s Kennedy Space Center in Florida. With 8.8 million pounds of thrust at liftoff, the American-built rocket propelled the crew inside the Orion spacecraft to space, delivering it to orbit with pinpoint accuracy after a smooth countdown conducted by the agency’s Artemis launch control team.

During the first day in space, the astronauts and teams on the ground checked out the spacecraft — named Integrity by the crew — to confirm all systems were healthy ahead of the transit to the Moon. NASA also deployed four CubeSats from international partners to Earth orbit.

On the second day of the test flight, with all systems Go, Orion’s service module fired its main engine, placing the astronauts on a trajectory that brought them 4,067 miles above the lunar surface at their closest approach.

“The Artemis II crew is home. The entry, descent, and landing systems performed as designed and the final test was completed as intended. This moment belongs to the thousands of people across fourteen countries who built, tested, and trusted this vehicle. Their work protected four human lives traveling at 25,000 miles per hour and brought them safely back to Earth,” said NASA Associate Administrator Amit Kshatriya. “Artemis II proved the vehicle, the teams, the architecture, and the international partnership that will return humanity to the lunar surface. Reid, Victor, Christina, and Jeremy carried the hopes of this world farther than humans have traveled in more than half a century. Fifty‑three years ago, humanity left the Moon. This time, we returned to stay. The future is ours to win.”

With astronauts aboard for the first time, engineers put Orion through a full in‑flight evaluation. The crew tested the spacecraft’s life support systems, confirming Orion can sustain humans in deep space. During several piloting demonstrations, crew members took manual control of the spacecraft, flying Orion to validate its handling and collect data that will guide future rendezvous and docking operations with human-rated landers during Artemis III and beyond.

The crew completed a series of tests to inform how NASA will fly future missions to the Moon, including evaluations of how the spacecraft operates during crew exercise, emergency equipment and procedures, the Orion crew survival system spacesuits, and other critical spacecraft systems.

Wiseman, Glover, Koch, and Hansen also supported scientific investigations to help NASA prepare astronauts to live and work on the Moon as the agency builds a Moon Base and looks toward Mars. These experiments — including the AVATAR investigation, which studies how human tissue responds to microgravity and the deep space radiation environment, and other human research performance studies — are gathering essential health data for long-duration missions.

During their April 6 lunar flyby, the astronauts captured more than 7,000 images of the lunar surface and a solar eclipse, during which the Moon blocked the Sun from Orion’s vantage point. The imagery includes striking views of earthset and earthrise, impact craters, ancient lava flows, our Milky Way galaxy, and surface fractures and color variations across the lunar terrain.

They documented the topography along the terminator — the boundary between lunar day and night — where low-angle sunlight casts long shadows across the surface, creating illumination conditions similar to those in the South Pole region where astronauts are scheduled to land in 2028. The crew also proposed potential names for two lunar craters and reported meteoroid impact flashes on the night side of the Moon.

Artemis II science will pave the way for future missions to the Moon’s surface by helping advance mission operations and training astronauts to use well-informed judgment to identify areas of high interest for science and exploration.

With the crew safely on Earth, NASA and its partners now will turn attention to preparing for next year’s Artemis III mission, when a new Orion crew will test integrated operations with commercially built Moon landers in low Earth orbit.

As part of a Golden Age of innovation and exploration, NASA will send Artemis astronauts on increasingly challenging missions to explore more of the Moon for scientific discovery, economic benefits, establish an enduring human presence on the lunar surface, and lay the groundwork for sending the first astronauts – American astronauts – to Mars.

To learn more about the Artemis program, visit:

https://www.nasa.gov/artemis

-end-

Bethany Stevens / Rachel Kraft

Headquarters, Washington

202-358-1600

bethany.c.stevens@nasa.gov / rachel.h.kraft@nasa.gov
articles.reasoning: The article reports a historic and successful NASA Artemis II mission, marking the first crewed lunar journey in over 50 years, setting a new distance record, and demonstrating key spacecraft capabilities. This achievement has broad significance for humanity's future space exploration, lunar presence, and eventual Mars missions. The detailed context and focus on the mission's accomplishments and future implications provide substantial substance.
articles.personality_title: NASA's Artemis II astronauts return safely after record-breaking Moon mission
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA recently launched Artemis II, the first mission with astronauts traveling near the Moon in over 50 years. This mission tested new spacecraft and systems needed for future Moon exploration.

**What happened** – On April 10, 2026, four astronauts—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch from NASA, and Jeremy Hansen from the Canadian Space Agency—returned safely to Earth after nearly 10 days in space. They flew aboard the Orion spacecraft launched by NASA’s powerful Space Launch System rocket. During their trip, they traveled farther from Earth than any humans have since 1970, completing a 252,756-mile journey around the Moon.

**Impact** – This mission proved that the new Orion spacecraft and rocket can carry humans safely to the Moon and back. The astronauts tested life support systems, practiced flying the spacecraft manually, and conducted experiments to learn how the body reacts to space conditions. They also took thousands of photos of the Moon’s surface and a solar eclipse, helping scientists prepare for future lunar exploration. Artemis II sets the stage for NASA’s plan to build a Moon base and eventually send astronauts to Mars.

**What's next step** – NASA is now focusing on Artemis III, planned for next year, which will include landing astronauts on the Moon’s surface using new Moon landers. The knowledge gained from Artemis II will help make those landings safer and more efficient. The Artemis program will continue sending crews on more challenging missions to explore the Moon and develop technologies for deep space.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s Artemis II mission successfully tested new spacecraft by sending astronauts farther into space than in decades, paving the way for future Moon landings and exploration beyond.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d'Artemis II de la NASA rentrent sur Terre après une mission record autour de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La NASA a récemment lancé Artemis II, la première mission avec des astronautes voyageant près de la Lune depuis plus de 50 ans. Cette mission a testé de nouveaux engins spatiaux et systèmes nécessaires pour l’exploration future de la Lune.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 10 avril 2026, quatre astronautes—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch de la NASA, et Jeremy Hansen de l’Agence spatiale canadienne—sont revenus sur Terre en toute sécurité après près de 10 jours dans l’espace. Ils ont voyagé à bord du vaisseau spatial Orion lancé par la puissante fusée Space Launch System de la NASA. Pendant leur voyage, ils ont parcouru la plus grande distance de la Terre atteinte par des humains depuis 1970, soit 252 756 miles autour de la Lune.

**Impact** – Cette mission a prouvé que le nouveau vaisseau Orion et la fusée peuvent transporter des humains en toute sécurité vers la Lune et revenir. Les astronautes ont testé les systèmes de survie, piloté manuellement le vaisseau et mené des expériences pour comprendre comment le corps réagit aux conditions spatiales. Ils ont aussi pris des milliers de photos de la surface lunaire et d’une éclipse solaire, aidant les scientifiques à préparer l’exploration lunaire future. Artemis II prépare la construction d’une base lunaire et des missions vers Mars.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA se concentre maintenant sur Artemis III, prévue pour l’année prochaine, qui comprendra l’atterrissage d’astronautes sur la surface lunaire avec de nouveaux engins d’atterrissage. Les connaissances acquises lors d’Artemis II aideront à rendre ces atterrissages plus sûrs et efficaces. Le programme Artemis continuera d’envoyer des équipages pour explorer plus loin la Lune et développer des technologies pour l’espace lointain.

**Message clé en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA a testé avec succès un nouveau vaisseau en envoyant des astronautes plus loin dans l’espace depuis des décennies, ouvrant la voie aux futurs atterrissages lunaires et à l’exploration au-delà.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II de la NASA regresan a la Tierra tras una misión récord alrededor de la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La NASA lanzó recientemente Artemis II, la primera misión con astronautas viajando cerca de la Luna en más de 50 años. Esta misión probó nuevas naves espaciales y sistemas necesarios para futuras exploraciones lunares.

**Qué pasó** – El 10 de abril de 2026, cuatro astronautas—Reid Wiseman, Victor Glover, Christina Koch de la NASA, y Jeremy Hansen de la Agencia Espacial Canadiense—regresaron a la Tierra sanos y salvos tras casi 10 días en el espacio. Viajaron a bordo de la nave Orion lanzada por el potente cohete Space Launch System de NASA. Durante su viaje, recorrieron la mayor distancia desde la Tierra alcanzada por humanos desde 1970, un total de 252,756 millas alrededor de la Luna.

**Impacto** – Esta misión demostró que la nueva nave Orion y el cohete pueden llevar humanos con seguridad a la Luna y de regreso. Los astronautas probaron los sistemas de soporte vital, pilotaron manualmente la nave y realizaron experimentos para entender cómo el cuerpo responde al espacio. También tomaron miles de fotos de la superficie lunar y un eclipse solar, ayudando a los científicos a preparar futuras exploraciones. Artemis II prepara la construcción de una base lunar y misiones a Marte.

**Próximo paso** – Ahora NASA se enfoca en Artemis III, planeada para el próximo año, que incluirá aterrizajes de astronautas en la superficie lunar con nuevos módulos de aterrizaje. Lo aprendido en Artemis II ayudará a hacer esos aterrizajes más seguros y eficientes. El programa Artemis seguirá enviando tripulaciones a misiones más desafiantes para explorar la Luna y desarrollar tecnologías para el espacio profundo.

**Frase clave** – La misión Artemis II de NASA probó con éxito una nueva nave al enviar astronautas más lejos en el espacio que en décadas, preparando el camino para futuros aterrizajes lunares y exploración más allá.
articles.image_prompt: A serene ocean scene at sunset with a stylized spacecraft gently floating on the water’s surface, surrounded by four glowing orbs representing the Artemis II astronauts, while a distant crescent Moon and a softly illuminated Earth rise over the horizon, all rendered in warm, natural tones with detailed, painterly brushstrokes.

articles.title: Gravitational waves may be hidden in the light atoms emit
articles.summary: Scientists have proposed a surprising new way to detect gravitational waves—by observing how they change the light emitted by atoms. These waves can subtly shift photon frequencies in different directions, leaving behind a detectable signature. The effect doesn’t change how much light atoms emit, which is why it’s gone unnoticed until now. If confirmed, this approach could lead to ultra-compact detectors using cold-atom systems.
articles.content:
Gravitational waves are tiny ripples in spacetime created by powerful cosmic events such as colliding black holes. Until now, scientists have detected them by measuring extremely small changes in distance using huge instruments that stretch for kilometers. A new theoretical study, accepted for publication in Physical Review Letters, suggests a very different strategy. Researchers from Stockholm University, Nordita, and the University of Tübingen propose looking at how these waves subtly alter the light emitted by atoms. While the idea is promising, it has not yet been tested experimentally.

Atoms that absorb energy do not stay excited for long. They quickly return to a lower energy state by releasing light at a specific frequency, a process known as spontaneous emission. This behavior comes from the atom's interaction with the quantum electromagnetic field.

"Gravitational waves modulate the quantum field, which in turn affects spontaneous emission," said Jerzy Paczos, a PhD student at Stockholm University. "This modulation can shift the frequencies of emitted photons compared with the no-wave case."

Hidden Signals in Directional Light

According to the researchers, gravitational waves would not change how often atoms emit light. Instead, they would subtly alter the frequency of the emitted photons depending on the direction in which they travel. Because the total emission rate stays the same, this effect has gone unnoticed until now.

The result would be a distinct directional pattern in the light's spectrum. This pattern could carry information about the gravitational wave's direction and polarization, offering a way to separate real signals from background noise.

Cold Atoms and Future Detectors

Detecting low-frequency gravitational waves is a major goal for future space missions. The team points out that systems based on atomic clocks, which rely on very precise optical transitions, could be especially useful. These systems allow for long interaction times, making cold-atom setups a strong candidate for testing the idea.

A Compact Alternative to Giant Instruments

The researchers compare atoms to a steady musical tone that normally sounds the same in every direction. A passing gravitational wave, however, would subtly change how that tone is heard depending on direction.

"Our findings may open a route toward compact gravitational-wave sensing, where the relevant atomic ensemble is millimeter-scale," said Navdeep Arya, a postdoctoral researcher at Stockholm University. "A thorough noise analysis is necessary to assess practical feasibility, but our first estimates are promising."

If confirmed, this approach could eventually lead to much smaller and more accessible detectors, offering a new way to observe some of the universe's most dramatic events.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific proposal for detecting gravitational waves through atomic light emission, which could lead to compact, accessible detectors. This represents a significant potential breakthrough in space science and observational technology with broad implications for astrophysics and cosmology.
articles.personality_title: Scientists propose detecting gravitational waves through atomic light shifts
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Gravitational waves are tiny ripples in space caused by huge events like black holes colliding. Right now, big machines kilometers long detect these waves by measuring tiny distance changes.

**What happened** – Researchers from Sweden and Germany suggested a new way to spot gravitational waves by watching how they change the light atoms give off. When atoms release light, they send out photons at certain frequencies. The waves can slightly shift these frequencies depending on the direction the light travels, creating a unique pattern.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it doesn’t need huge machines. Instead, it could use very small setups with cold atoms to detect gravitational waves. The effect changes the light’s color pattern, not how much light is made, which is why it hasn’t been noticed before.

**What's next step** – Scientists will need to test this idea in experiments, especially using atomic clocks and cold atoms, which can measure light very precisely. If it works, it could lead to compact detectors that are easier to build and use than current large instruments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new method could detect gravitational waves by spotting tiny shifts in light from atoms, paving the way for smaller and simpler detectors.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques proposent de détecter les ondes gravitationnelles grâce aux changements de lumière atomique
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les ondes gravitationnelles sont de petites ondulations dans l’espace créées par de grands événements comme la collision de trous noirs. Aujourd’hui, on les détecte avec de très grandes machines qui mesurent de minuscules variations de distance.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de Suède et d’Allemagne ont proposé une nouvelle méthode pour détecter ces ondes en observant comment elles modifient la lumière émise par les atomes. Quand un atome émet de la lumière, il envoie des photons à certaines fréquences. Les ondes gravitationnelles peuvent légèrement décaler ces fréquences selon la direction de la lumière, créant un motif unique.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle ne nécessite pas de grandes machines. Elle pourrait utiliser de petits dispositifs avec des atomes froids pour détecter les ondes. L’effet change le motif des couleurs de la lumière, pas sa quantité, ce qui explique pourquoi il est passé inaperçu.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques doivent tester cette idée en laboratoire, notamment avec des horloges atomiques et des atomes froids, qui mesurent la lumière très précisément. Si cela fonctionne, cela pourrait aboutir à des détecteurs compacts, plus faciles à fabriquer et à utiliser.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle méthode pourrait détecter les ondes gravitationnelles en observant de petits décalages dans la lumière des atomes, ouvrant la voie à des détecteurs plus petits et simples.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos proponen detectar ondas gravitacionales mediante cambios en la luz atómica
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las ondas gravitacionales son pequeñas ondulaciones en el espacio causadas por grandes eventos como la colisión de agujeros negros. Actualmente, se detectan con máquinas enormes que miden cambios muy pequeños en distancias.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de Suecia y Alemania sugirieron una nueva forma de detectar estas ondas observando cómo cambian la luz que emiten los átomos. Cuando un átomo emite luz, envía fotones con ciertas frecuencias. Las ondas gravitacionales pueden cambiar un poco estas frecuencias según la dirección de la luz, creando un patrón especial.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque no necesita máquinas gigantes. Podría usar aparatos muy pequeños con átomos fríos para detectar las ondas. El efecto cambia el patrón de color de la luz, no la cantidad, por eso no se había visto antes.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos deben probar esta idea en experimentos, usando relojes atómicos y átomos fríos, que miden la luz con mucha precisión. Si funciona, podría crear detectores compactos, más fáciles de construir y usar.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo método podría detectar ondas gravitacionales observando pequeños cambios en la luz de los átomos, lo que permitiría detectores más pequeños y simples.
articles.image_prompt: A delicate cluster of glowing atoms emitting soft, shimmering light waves that ripple gently outward, subtly bending and shifting in a graceful, directional pattern against a calm cosmic backdrop of muted blues and warm neutrals, symbolizing the hidden influence of gravitational waves on atomic light.

articles.title: Scientists think dark matter might come in two forms
articles.summary: A mysterious glow of gamma rays at the center of the Milky Way has long hinted at dark matter, but the lack of similar signals in smaller dwarf galaxies has cast doubt on that idea. Now, researchers propose a bold twist: dark matter might not be a single particle at all, but a mix of two different types that must interact with each other to produce detectable signals.
articles.content:
Sometimes, not seeing something can be just as important as detecting it. That idea is at the heart of a new study published in the Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics (JCAP). The research suggests scientists may not need to find identical signals everywhere in the universe to understand dark matter.

The study focuses on a puzzling observation. Astronomers have detected an excess of gamma radiation at the center of the Milky Way, which could be produced when dark matter particles collide and annihilate. However, similar signals have not been found in other places, such as dwarf galaxies. According to the new work, that absence does not necessarily rule out dark matter as the source.

Instead, dark matter itself may be more complex than previously thought. Rather than a single type of particle, it could consist of multiple components that behave differently depending on their environment.

The Milky Way's Gamma-Ray Excess

Dark matter is believed to make up a large portion of the universe, yet it has never been directly observed. Scientists infer its existence from the way its gravity affects visible matter. Despite decades of research, its true nature remains unknown.

Many leading theories describe dark matter as being made of particles. In some models, when two of these particles meet, they annihilate and produce high-energy radiation such as gamma rays. Detecting this radiation is one of the main strategies scientists use to search for dark matter.

"Right now there seems to be an excess of photons coming from an approximately spherical region surrounding the disk of the Milky Way," explains Gordan Krnjaic, a theoretical physicist at the Fermi National Accelerator Laboratory (Fermilab) in the United States and one of the study's authors. Observations from the Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope have revealed this unusual glow, which could be linked to dark matter. Still, other explanations are possible, including gamma rays produced by astrophysical sources like pulsars.

To better understand the origin of this signal, scientists look beyond our galaxy. "If certain theories of dark matter are true, we should see it in every galaxy, for example in every dwarf galaxy," explains Krnjaic.

Why Dwarf Galaxies Matter

Dwarf galaxies are small, faint systems that contain large amounts of dark matter. Because they have relatively few stars and less background radiation, they provide a cleaner setting to search for dark matter signals.

Standard particle-based models of dark matter typically describe two main possibilities for how annihilation occurs. In the simplest scenario, the likelihood of annihilation is constant and does not depend on how fast the particles are moving. If this is true, a signal seen in the Milky Way should also appear in other dark matter-rich systems, including dwarf galaxies.

In another scenario, the annihilation rate depends on particle velocity. Since dark matter particles move slowly within galaxies, this type of interaction would make annihilation extremely rare, leaving little or no detectable signal anywhere.

Under these conventional frameworks, the lack of gamma-ray emission from dwarf galaxies makes it harder to interpret the Milky Way's gamma-ray excess as evidence of dark matter.

A Two-Component Dark Matter Model

Krnjaic and his collaborators propose a different explanation that could resolve this tension. Their model suggests dark matter may consist of two distinct types of particles rather than one.

"What we're trying to point out in this paper is that you could have a different kind of environmental dependence, even if the annihilation probability is constant in the center of the galaxy," explains Krnjaic. "Dark matter could straightforwardly be two different particles, and the two different particles need to find each other in order to annihilate."

In this picture, the chance of annihilation depends not only on how often particles interact, but also on the balance between the two types of dark matter in a given system. That balance may vary from one galaxy to another. In a galaxy like the Milky Way, the two particle types could exist in similar amounts, making interactions more likely. In dwarf galaxies, one type might dominate, reducing the chance of collisions and limiting any detectable signal.

"In this way, you get very different predictions for the emission," explains Krnjaic.

What Future Observations Could Reveal

This two-component model offers a more flexible way to interpret current observations. It allows scientists to explain why the Milky Way shows a gamma-ray signal while dwarf galaxies do not, without discarding the possibility that dark matter is responsible.

Future observations will be crucial for testing this idea. The Fermi Gamma-ray Telescope may provide more detailed data on dwarf galaxies, where measurements are still limited. Detecting gamma rays in these systems could indicate a similar mix of dark matter components. On the other hand, continued non-detection might suggest that one component is less common in those environments.

Even so, the interpretation is not straightforward. Other astrophysical factors could influence what is observed, meaning scientists will need to compare this model with a broad range of data.

The paper "dSph-obic dark matter" by Asher Berlin, Joshua Foster, Dan Hooper and Gordan Krnjaic is now available in JCAP.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific advancement proposing a novel two-component dark matter model that could resolve existing observational puzzles about gamma-ray signals in the Milky Way and dwarf galaxies. This breakthrough enhances understanding of dark matter, a fundamental but elusive component of the universe, with broad implications for astrophysics and cosmology. The study is detailed, focused, and offers a new framework that can guide future observations and experiments, representing meaningful progress in space science.
articles.personality_title: Scientists propose dark matter may consist of two interacting particles
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Dark matter is a mysterious substance that makes up much of the universe, but scientists have never seen it directly. They study it by looking at its effects, such as unusual gamma-ray signals detected in the center of our galaxy, the Milky Way. However, these signals are missing in smaller nearby galaxies called dwarf galaxies, creating a puzzle.

**What happened** – A new study suggests that dark matter might not be made of just one kind of particle, but two different types that must interact to produce the gamma rays we detect. This idea helps explain why the Milky Way shows a gamma-ray glow while dwarf galaxies do not. The research was published in the Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics by scientists including Gordan Krnjaic from Fermilab.

**Impact** – This two-particle model offers a fresh way to understand dark matter’s behavior in different galaxies. It shows that the amount and interaction of the two particles can vary by galaxy, which changes how often they collide and create gamma rays. This helps solve the mystery of why signals appear in the Milky Way but not in dwarf galaxies, improving how scientists interpret data.

**What's next step** – Future observations with the Fermi Gamma-ray Space Telescope and other instruments will test this model by searching for gamma rays in dwarf galaxies and other locations. These studies will help confirm if dark matter really comes in two forms and how they interact, guiding new experiments and theories.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists now think dark matter could be made of two types of particles whose interactions explain why gamma-ray signals appear in some galaxies but not others.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques proposent que la matière noire soit composée de deux particules interagissant
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La matière noire est une substance mystérieuse qui compose une grande partie de l’univers, mais que les scientifiques n’ont jamais observée directement. Ils l’étudient en examinant ses effets, comme des signaux inhabituels de rayons gamma détectés au centre de notre galaxie, la Voie lactée. Cependant, ces signaux manquent dans de petites galaxies proches appelées galaxies naines, ce qui crée une énigme.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une nouvelle étude suggère que la matière noire ne serait pas composée d’un seul type de particule, mais de deux types différents qui doivent interagir pour produire les rayons gamma détectés. Cette idée aide à expliquer pourquoi la Voie lactée montre une lueur de rayons gamma alors que les galaxies naines ne l’ont pas. La recherche a été publiée dans le Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics par des scientifiques dont Gordan Krnjaic du Fermilab.

**Impact** – Ce modèle à deux particules offre une nouvelle façon de comprendre le comportement de la matière noire dans différentes galaxies. Il montre que la quantité et l’interaction des deux particules peuvent varier selon la galaxie, ce qui modifie la fréquence de leurs collisions et la production de rayons gamma. Cela aide à résoudre le mystère des signaux présents dans la Voie lactée mais absents dans les galaxies naines, améliorant ainsi l’interprétation des données par les scientifiques.

**Prochaine étape** – Les futures observations avec le télescope spatial Fermi Gamma-ray et d’autres instruments testeront ce modèle en recherchant des rayons gamma dans les galaxies naines et d’autres endroits. Ces études aideront à confirmer si la matière noire existe vraiment sous deux formes et comment elles interagissent, guidant de nouvelles expériences et théories.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Les scientifiques pensent désormais que la matière noire pourrait être composée de deux types de particules dont les interactions expliquent pourquoi les signaux de rayons gamma apparaissent dans certaines galaxies mais pas dans d’autres.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos proponen que la materia oscura podría estar formada por dos partículas que interactúan
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La materia oscura es una sustancia misteriosa que forma gran parte del universo, pero los científicos nunca la han visto directamente. La estudian observando sus efectos, como señales inusuales de rayos gamma detectadas en el centro de nuestra galaxia, la Vía Láctea. Sin embargo, estas señales no aparecen en galaxias pequeñas cercanas llamadas galaxias enanas, lo que genera un misterio.

**Qué pasó** – Un nuevo estudio sugiere que la materia oscura podría no estar hecha de un solo tipo de partícula, sino de dos tipos diferentes que deben interactuar para producir los rayos gamma que detectamos. Esta idea ayuda a explicar por qué la Vía Láctea muestra un brillo de rayos gamma mientras que las galaxias enanas no. La investigación fue publicada en el Journal of Cosmology and Astroparticle Physics por científicos, entre ellos Gordan Krnjaic del Fermilab.

**Impacto** – Este modelo de dos partículas ofrece una nueva forma de entender el comportamiento de la materia oscura en distintas galaxias. Muestra que la cantidad y la interacción de las dos partículas pueden variar según la galaxia, lo que cambia la frecuencia de sus colisiones y la producción de rayos gamma. Esto ayuda a resolver el misterio de por qué las señales aparecen en la Vía Láctea pero no en las galaxias enanas, mejorando la interpretación de los datos por parte de los científicos.

**Próximo paso** – Las futuras observaciones con el telescopio espacial de rayos gamma Fermi y otros instrumentos probarán este modelo buscando rayos gamma en galaxias enanas y otros lugares. Estos estudios ayudarán a confirmar si la materia oscura realmente está formada por dos tipos y cómo interactúan, guiando nuevos experimentos y teorías.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Los científicos ahora creen que la materia oscura podría estar formada por dos tipos de partículas cuyas interacciones explican por qué las señales de rayos gamma aparecen en algunas galaxias pero no en otras.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cosmic scene showing two distinct glowing orbs—one blue and one gold—intertwined and gently orbiting each other at the center of a stylized Milky Way galaxy, surrounded by smaller, dimmer orbs representing dwarf galaxies where only one orb type predominates, all rendered in soft, natural tones with a subtle gamma-ray aura illuminating the scene.

articles.title: New era for space dawns as Artemis astronauts return
articles.summary: After a 10-day journey around the far side of the Moon, the four astronauts on NASA’s Artemis II mission are set to splash down in the Pacific Ocean late on Friday.
articles.content:
The historic mission, which set a record during its lunar flyby for how far humans have ventured into space, was a significant step towards a return to the Moon's surface for the first time since 1972 – as well as laying the foundation for future space exploration.

The UN’s advocate for space, internationally acclaimed physicist, author and broadcaster Professor Brian Cox told the UN: “It’s not just about exploration anymore, it’s about making everybody’s life better.”

Life-enhancing

The Artemis programme aims to establish a sustainable human presence on the Moon, explore the lunar South Pole, and test technologies for future crewed missions to Mars.

“As we move out into space, the thing to remember is that we are not escaping the planet, and the planet is not becoming less important...we are doing it to enhance our lives on this planet,” Prof. Cox said, emphasising that Earth is the best planet in the universe for us because it’s where we evolved.

As the United Nations marks this historic milestone on the International Day of Human Space Flight, the UN’s Champion for Space, Professor Brian Cox, wants to show the world that “space is no longer science fiction,” but rather an “important part of all our lives and our economy.”

Soundcloud

Space boosting life on Earth

Space technology has the potential to address some of the biggest challenges facing our planet; these include climate change, disaster management and sustainable development.

“More and more of the things we take for granted here on Earth are becoming part of the space-based economy,” Prof. Cox highlighted.

Satellite imagery gives us a clearer view of the challenges we face on Earth, such as monitoring crop yields, managing water resources, and tracking deforestation in real time.

Meanwhile, satellite connectivity can bridge the data divide by supporting e-learning for isolated schools or enabling telemedicine in remote areas.

“It’s not just about exploration anymore; it’s about making everybody’s life better,” he continued.

Space for all

The Champion for Space wants to share his passion for space with the world, reminding us that space ‘belongs to everyone.’

Prof. Cox shines a spotlight on the role of the UN beyond the stratosphere as a convenor, a gateway to space, and a capacity-builder for nations to peacefully explore and use space.

“Space brings people together because there are no borders there,” he said.

UNOOSA, the UN body for promoting international cooperation in outer space, provides a platform for consensus-building among Member States, including over potentially thorny issues, such as lunar cooperation, space traffic, space resources, and planetary defence.

Space savvy

“There has always been an idealism to space, but it’s not a naïve idealism,” Prof. Cox emphasises.

He recalls in particular the 1975 Apollo–Soyuz Mission, a major moment of scientific and political cooperation between the United States and the Soviet Union, and the first time a spacecraft of two nations docked together in orbit.

“It was unthinkable that the United States and the Soviet Union, as it then was, would collaborate to dock in space, and develop technologies for collaboration that we are still using today,” he said

A UN flag was among the items exchanged between the astronauts to recognise all humankind’s achievements in space.

JAXA/NASA MIR-SAT1: the first ever satellite launched by Mauritius, deployed from the International Space Station.

Shared benefits

“Ensuring everybody can contribute and benefit ultimately makes our voyage outwards into space easier,” says Prof. Cox. “It will happen more rapidly if everybody is collaborating together, and by nature it will benefit everybody. It’s a new frontier.”

Recently, UNOOSA along with its partners have helped countries including Kenya, Mauritius, Moldova and Guatemala deploy their first satellites, and

as more countries join the space community, it has assisted emerging space nations in drafting national space laws that align with international obligations.

Threat of space junk

Space is vast, but it is becoming increasingly crowded. In 2025, over 4,500 new satellites were launched, by contrast in 2015, it was just over 200.

Many space objects fragment and create new junk, with estimates suggesting there are as many as 130 million pieces of debris in Earths orbit.

Even very small pieces less than one centimetre in size can cause catastrophic damage, threatening to lock us into a "Kessler Syndrome" where collisions create more debris, leading to a cascade of further collisions.

UN Photo/Manuel Elías

As we develop more infrastructure in space, and come to rely on it more on Earth, “this becomes more and more of a serious problem,” Cox warns.

A UN flag on Mars?

Whilst recognising the practical ways that space can enhance our daily lives, Cox does not want to “take the romance away from space”.

The physicist added that the discovery he would most like to see in his lifetime would be “another living world.”

He is “sure there is life out there somewhere,” adding: “I would not be surprised if there were microbes on many of the moons in the solar system, and possibly also on Mars. I would, however, be surprised if there’s any complex life in the solar system, very surprised.”

In addition to another Moon landing, he hopes that one day, humans will set foot on Mars, an opportunity to emphasise that space belongs to all humankind.

“I hope that when we plant a flag on the surface of Mars, it will be a United Nations flag.”
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the successful Artemis II mission, a historic human spaceflight milestone that extends human presence beyond the Moon, with broad implications for future space exploration and tangible benefits on Earth through space technology. It highlights international cooperation, sustainable lunar exploration, and the potential for space advancements to address global challenges such as climate change and connectivity. The story is focused, detailed, and emphasizes the positive impact of space exploration on humanity and Earth.
articles.personality_title: Artemis II astronauts complete historic Moon mission, opening new space chapter
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – NASA’s Artemis II mission marks the first time humans have traveled around the far side of the Moon since 1972. This mission aims to prepare for a return to the Moon’s surface and future space exploration.

**What happened** – Four astronauts spent 10 days orbiting the Moon’s far side before safely splashing down in the Pacific Ocean on Friday. The mission set a record for how far humans have traveled into space and tested important technologies for future trips to the Moon and Mars.

**Impact** – This mission is important because it moves us closer to living and working on the Moon, especially near the lunar South Pole. It also shows how space technology can help us on Earth by improving climate monitoring, disaster management, and internet access in remote areas. The United Nations supports peaceful cooperation in space and helps countries join the space community.

**What's next step** – Plans include establishing a lasting human presence on the Moon and preparing for crewed missions to Mars. Countries are working together to manage space safely as it becomes more crowded with satellites and debris.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The Artemis II mission is a key step toward sustainable Moon exploration and shows how space benefits life on Earth through international teamwork and technology.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les astronautes d'Artemis II achèvent une mission historique autour de la Lune
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La mission Artemis II de la NASA est la première à faire voyager des humains autour de la face cachée de la Lune depuis 1972. Cette mission prépare un retour sur la surface lunaire et une exploration spatiale future.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Quatre astronautes ont passé 10 jours en orbite autour de la face cachée de la Lune avant d’amerrir en toute sécurité dans l’océan Pacifique vendredi. La mission a établi un record de distance parcourue par des humains dans l’espace et testé des technologies clés pour de futurs voyages vers la Lune et Mars.

**Impact** – Cette mission est importante car elle rapproche la possibilité de vivre et travailler durablement sur la Lune, notamment au pôle Sud lunaire. Elle montre aussi comment la technologie spatiale aide la Terre en améliorant la surveillance du climat, la gestion des catastrophes et l’accès à Internet dans les zones isolées. L’ONU soutient la coopération pacifique dans l’espace et aide les pays à rejoindre la communauté spatiale.

**Prochaine étape** – Il est prévu d’établir une présence humaine durable sur la Lune et de préparer des missions habitées vers Mars. Les pays collaborent pour gérer l’espace en toute sécurité, alors qu’il devient plus encombré avec des satellites et des débris.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – La mission Artemis II est une étape clé vers une exploration lunaire durable et montre comment l’espace profite à la vie sur Terre grâce à la coopération internationale et à la technologie.
articles.personality_title_es: Los astronautas de Artemis II completan misión histórica alrededor de la Luna
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La misión Artemis II de la NASA es la primera vez que humanos viajan alrededor del lado oculto de la Luna desde 1972. Esta misión prepara el regreso a la superficie lunar y la exploración espacial futura.

**Qué pasó** – Cuatro astronautas pasaron 10 días orbitando el lado oculto de la Luna antes de aterrizar con seguridad en el Océano Pacífico el viernes. La misión estableció un récord de la distancia que los humanos han viajado en el espacio y probó tecnologías importantes para futuros viajes a la Luna y Marte.

**Impacto** – Esta misión es importante porque acerca la posibilidad de vivir y trabajar en la Luna de forma sostenible, especialmente en el polo sur lunar. También muestra cómo la tecnología espacial puede ayudar en la Tierra mejorando el monitoreo del clima, la gestión de desastres y el acceso a internet en áreas remotas. Las Naciones Unidas apoyan la cooperación pacífica en el espacio y ayudan a los países a unirse a la comunidad espacial.

**Próximo paso** – Se planea establecer una presencia humana duradera en la Luna y preparar misiones tripuladas a Marte. Los países trabajan juntos para manejar el espacio de forma segura, ya que se está volviendo más lleno de satélites y desechos.

**Resumen en una frase** – La misión Artemis II es un paso clave hacia la exploración sostenible de la Luna y muestra cómo el espacio beneficia la vida en la Tierra mediante la cooperación internacional y la tecnología.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing lunar horizon with a stylized spacecraft gently orbiting above, a large, softly illuminated Earth rising in the background symbolizing home, and a delicate, translucent United Nations flag unfurled on the Moon’s surface, all rendered in natural, muted earth tones and deep space blues.
